coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible carbon dots derived from κ-carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid for dual modality sensing platform of sugar and its anti-diabetic drug release behavior
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Poushali
Maity, Priti Prasanna
Ganguly, Sayan
Ghosh, Sabyasachi
Baral, Joydeep
Bose, Madhuparna
Choudhary, Sumita
Gangopadhyay, Subhashis
Dhara, Santanu
Das, Amit Kumar
Banerjee, Susanta
Das, Narayan Chandra
coredata.dc:description: Detection of sugar by enzymatic assay has been suffering from costly, time-taking, instable and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Recently, chemosensors that have affinity towards boronate became the hot topic in the domain of monosaccharide detection. In this work, a novel strategy was addressed to fabricate carbon dots (C-dots) from linear sulfated polysaccharides κ- carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid for nonenzymatic monosaccharide (glucose) detection. The boronic acid group anchored C-dots surface can form assembly by covalently bonded with the cis-diol moiety of the glucose which caused fluorescence quenching of the C-dots. The inert surface nature of the luminescent C-dots enables them to sense as low as 1.7 μM glucose without the interference of other biomolecules. The proposed sensing system was successfully applied for assay of glucose in blood serum. Interestingly, these C-dots were used as a nano vehicle for delivery of anti-diabetic drug Metformin. Good biocompatibility results were found with MTT and hemolysis assay. Owing to its simplicity and effectiveness, the as-prepared C-dots offered great promise for blood sugar diagnosis and treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Glucose sensor
Drug delivery
Hemolysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro hemolysis assay of BSC-dots
Interaction between the C-dots and Glucose
Optimization of the conditions effecting for determination of glucose
Performance of BSC-dots for glucose sensing
Selectivity of the fluorescent probe for glucose
Application of BSC-dots for determination of glucose in human serum
Data fittings of drug release to model equations
In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the BSC-dots nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the BSC-dots. (b) Particle size distribution histogram of the BSC-dots. (c) AFM image of the BSC-dots. (d) Height profile of the AFM image.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the BSC-dots (b) XPS scanning spectrum of the BSC-dots. XPS high resolution survey scan of (c) C1s and (d) B1s (e) S2p of the BSC-dots.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the BSC-dots in aqueous dispersion, inset: photograph taken under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (b) Emission spectra of the KLBC-dots at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Up conversion behavior of the BSC-dots. (d) schematic illustrations of normal PL and upconverted PL.

(a) Concentration dependent emission spectra of the BSC-dots (b) Schematic of the concentration tuned behavior of the C-dots. (c) pH dependent property of the BSC-dots (d) FL decay profile of the as-prepared BSC-dots.

Photostability of the BSC-dots (a) Long-term photostability of the BSC-dots (b) Images of solution containing BSC-dots under UV illumination before (left) and after (right) two months. (c) Stability of the BSC-dots under various physiological conditions (d) Effect of UV exposure time (e) Temperature on the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots. (f) Influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots.

(a) TEM image of the BSC-dots in presence of glucose (2100 μM) (b) The fluorescence spectra of the BSC-dots before and after the addition of 1400 μM glucose. (c) Influence of incubation time on the fluorescence intensities of the BSC-dot system. (d) Schematic representation for working principle of BSC-dots binding to glucose molecule. Other groups on surface of BSC-dots are omitted for clarity in B.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of the BSC-dots at different glucose concentrations (b) The dependence of F 0/F with the concentration of glucose. Inset showed the linear plot of the decreased intensity of the BSC-dots as a function of glucose concentration (c) The BSC-dots based system at different saccharides (concentration: 210 μM) (d) The effects of potential biological interfering substances on fluorescence of BSC-dots aqueous solution. The concentrations of various subtances from left to right were 21 μM, 0.13 nM, 0.65 μM, 4.13 μM, 4.20 μM, 5.9 μM, 3.20 μM, 4.15 μM, 7.91 μM, 13.10 μM, 2.82 μM, 14.10 μM, 5.23 μM, 10.14 μM, 8.32 μM, 0.45 μM, 14.80 μM, 2.43 μM, 20 μM; Glucose concentration was 210 μM. F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the BSC-dots in the absence and presence of the corresponding substances, respectively (e) Images of BSC-dots tethered paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different concentration of glucose (left to right the concentrations were 0, 140, 210, 420, 700, 1400, and 2100 μM) (f) Images of BSC-dots tethered paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different biological substances.

(a) UV visible spectra of pristine drug, BSC-dots and drug loaded BSC-dots (b) Fluorescence spectra of BSC-dots and drug loaded BSC-dots. (c) Schematic representation of the surface chemistry drug loading interaction integration of BSC-dots with Metformin molecules in different pH media (d) Cumulative release plot for control experiment and drug release plot for other environments (e) Zero order kinetic model fittings for timed release plot (f) Higuchi model fittings (g) Koresmeter-Peppas model fitting.

(a) Quantitative plot for MTT assay (b) Fluorescent microscopic images of MG-63 cells after 5 days of study for control experiment (c) Fluorescent microscopic images of MG-63 cells after 5 days of study for C-dots (d) Hemolysis assay with different concentrations (ii) 100 μg/mL (iii) 400 μg/mL (iv) 700 μg/mL of BSC-dots, (v) PBS (10 mM) and (i) Triton X-100 (1%, v/v) acted as negative and positive control, respectively.
Representation of covalent binding between boronic acid and glucose.
One step synthesis of C-dots from biopolymer kappa carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid.
Comparison of synthesis, particle size, optical properties (absorbance, excitation, emission, PL colour, QY), and application of the BSC-dots with the reported C-dots from various precursors.
Comparison of the analytical performances of glucose sensing between the present method and other reported sensors.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible carbon dots derived from κ-carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid for dual modality sensing platform of sugar and its anti-diabetic drug release behavior

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Birla Institute of Technology & Science, Pilani, Rajasthan 333031, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of sugar by enzymatic assay has been suffering from costly, time-taking, instable and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Recently, chemosensors that have affinity towards boronate became the hot topic in the domain of monosaccharide detection. In this work, a novel strategy was addressed to fabricate carbon dots (C-dots) from linear sulfated polysaccharides κ- carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid for nonenzymatic monosaccharide (glucose) detection. The boronic acid group anchored C-dots surface can form assembly by covalently bonded with the cis-diol moiety of the glucose which caused fluorescence quenching of the C-dots. The inert surface nature of the luminescent C-dots enables them to sense as low as 1.7 μM glucose without the interference of other biomolecules. The proposed sensing system was successfully applied for assay of glucose in blood serum. Interestingly, these C-dots were used as a nano vehicle for delivery of anti-diabetic drug Metformin. Good biocompatibility results were found with MTT and hemolysis assay. Owing to its simplicity and effectiveness, the as-prepared C-dots offered great promise for blood sugar diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a chronic disease and without proper control it can lead to severe health complications such as kidney failure, cardiovascular disease, and blindness. In this 21st century, with the progress of living standards, diabetes mellitus has increased in fast pace all over the globe due to the large amount of high-sugar consumption. According to the statistics of the World Health Organization (WHO), in 2010 about 300 million persons suffered from diabetes and this number is projected to be almost double in 2030 [1,2]. We know, one of the major challenges is good diabetic control for most diabetic patients to maintain good health. Therefore, monitoring of glucose concentration in blood is required to detect hypo or hyper-glycemia and to adjust these deviations for maintaining long-term near-normoglycemia [3]. In this context, glucose sensors have attracted considerable research interest.

Clinically used glucose sensors are enzyme based and apply glucose oxidase for sensing to maintain blood glucose level. Enzymatic assay for glucose sensing have made good development in last three years including electrochemistry [4], electrocatalysis [5], chemiluminescence [6], Raman scattering [7], and so on. Regardless of their widespread application, enzyme-based sensors suffered from several limitations. This include huge difference in results responsible on the local oxygen concentration, feeding of the analyte of interest from samples, low thermal stability, and decreased activity with time due to loss of functional enzyme produced by denaturation [8]. Considering these limitations, it is important and crucial to develop nonenzyme based glucose sensors to overcome the above defects. System like cyclodextrin modified gold nano particles [9], carbon coated magnetite nanoparticles [10] exhibited catalytic activity and concurrently possessed imitating actions of both glucose oxidase and hydrogen peroxidase. Another strategy considered to be suitable for non-enzyme based glucose sensors is boronate affinity approach. Boronic acid derivatives were widely used in synthesis and design of colour sensors [11], carbohydrate transporters [12], chromatographic materials [13] etc. The boronic acid derivatives showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards monosaccharides due to their ability to covalently bond to cis-diol moiety (Scheme 1 ) in basic pH. This phenomenon relies on the thermodynamic property of boronic acid to form complex with a cis-diol conformation [14]. Moreover, boronic acid conjugated to any fluorophore could alter the emission of fluorophore which might cause alteration in decay time, intensity, and polarization [15,16]. Due to this reasons, boronic acid derivatives offered good tool for the improvement of molecular probes for monosaccharides [17]. Recently, carbon dots (C-dots), a new member of the carbon nanofamily attracting immense interest to the researchers due to their interesting characteristics, such as high photostability, tunable excitation and emission, biocompatibility, etc. [18–22]. These outstanding performances endowed them as a promising applicant in sensing, [23–28] catalysis, [29,30] bioimaging, [31–34] and light emitting device [35] etc. Due to these properties C-dots are superior to the other fluorescence nanoparticles, like semiconductor quantum dots and noble metal nanoparticles. Researchers are developing green synthesis pathway to prepare cost-effective C-dots [36]. Still, C-dots prepared from polysaccharide such as chitosan [37], chitin [38], alginate [39], kappa-carrageenan [40] are not much explored yet.

In this work, we proposed an easy-to-sense platform for determining blood sugar without any catalytic/enzymatic mediated process. The chemosensor for this respective work was carried out by boronic acid functionalized C-dots. The choice of the precursor i.e. phenyl boronic acid had been chosen due to their excellent compatibilization towards π-electron cloud rich carbon core of C-dots and glucose recognized terminal. The naturally occurring kappa-carrageenan played the role of carbon source and thermally coupled to phenyl boronic acid was acted as surface ligand for efficaciously glucose binding. The BSC-dots showed sensitive and selective glucose sensing not only in solution but also in paper based strips. The newly synthesized BSC-dots had been applied as fluorescent sensing platform for glucose molecules present in blood having wide window of quenching domain (0–2100 μM). Besides such activity towards chemical recognition of blood sugar, the prepared C-dots were also experimented against drug attachment. For drug loading and delivery study, herein we chose an anti-diabetic drug which was easily anchored by the surface ligands. The drug binding and delivery suggested excellent nano-carrier like performance of C-dots in aquatic environment. The MTT and hemolysis assay was performed which showed good biocompatibility of BSC-dots. The major throughput of our work is that the sensing and drug delivery both the functions have been executed here. Therefore, these nanoprobe were envisioned to be potentially superior than the presently used glucose treatment and monitoring methods.

Kappa Carrageenan (99.9%), quinine sulfate (>99.0%), Phenyl boronic acid (>97.0%), and 3-(4,5-Dime-thylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. All other chemicals used were of reagent grade.

The C-dots were prepared through a simple one step hydrothermal treatment. 0.2 g kappa carrageenan and 0.25 g of phenyl boronic acid were added to 20 mL distilled water and stirred for 15 min to get a homogeneous solution. After that, the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL dried Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and we get deep orange colour product (BSC-dots). To remove large carbon particles, the solution was then filtered using a filtration membrane (0.22 μm of pore size). The sample was freeze-dried in order to get the powder form for further characterizations. The one step synthesis of the BSC-dots was illustrated in Scheme 2 .

200 μL of BSC-dots (1 mg/mL) dispersion was diluted to 3 mL with 0.1 M PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4). Then different concentrations of glucose (0–2100 μM) were added and mixed thoroughly. After thirty minutes of reaction, the fluorescence measurements were performed.
Collection of blood samples and procedures for glucose detection.

For real sample assay, blood sample was collected from normal healthy volunteer donor, with informed consent. The sample was collected in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) vacutainer tubes and centrifuged at 900 rpm for 20 min. Afterward, 550 μL of the supernatant serum (not mixed with blood cells) was dispensed into a polypropylene tube and stored in a refrigerator for further use. Prior to determination of glucose in blood serum, the sample was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 30 min. Afterthat, the supernatant serum was diluted with 3 mL of 0.1 M PBS solution. 200 μL of BSC-dots solution was added to the serum sample. Then, the mixture was mixed thoroughly and spiked with various concentrations of glucose and analyzed under the identical condition mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis assay of BSC-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The hemolytic assay of the BSC-dots was evaluated as per the following procedure. Fresh blood sample was taken in 5 mL EDTA coated containers. Red blood cells (RBC) were collected by blood centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 7 min followed by thorough washing by PBS. After that RBCs were dispersed into PBS followed by addition of BSC-dots (1 mL) into that mixture. The BSC-dots were varied from 100 μg/mL to 700 μg/mL and incubated for 3 h at ambient temperature. Similar kind of experiments was performed by 1 vol% Triton X-100 and 10 mM PBS for positive and negative controls respectively. The obtained supernatant of the incubated mixtures was tested in UV-spectrophotometer at 450, 380, and 415 nm for absorbance values. The hemoglobin release (% hemolysis) was calculated by using the following equation.

Anti-diabetic drug metformin is a common medication for diabetic mellitus since several years. Herein, metformin hydrochloride (Met; C4H11N5.HCl) has been chosen as a model drug for in vitro drug release study. In order to prepare the metformin loaded BSC-dots (Met-C-dots), at first the aqueous solution of drug was prepared by dissolving 200 mg of Metformin in 500 mL of Milli-Q water. After preparing the drug solution, the dissolved drug was stored in 4 °C with paraffin stretch wrap film. After that BSC-dots dispersion was prepared taking 50 mg freeze dried BSC-dots in 100 mL Milli-Q water. Then the drug solution and BSC-dots dispersion was taken in a glass beaker and stirred overnight (10−12h). For protection from diffused sunlight, the system was cover by aluminium foil to present unwanted oxidation of drug. After completion of stirring, the Met-C-dots mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min. and transferred into dialysis bag (MWCO 10 kDa). The dialysis was carried out for next 48 h. For quantitative estimation of drug loading, the aliquot had been collected followed by calibration plot at 233 nm (λmax for Metformin). The efficiency of drug loading (DLE) was measured as per the given equation;
DLE
%
=
Amount of
Met − C − dots
−
Amount of free Metformin
amount of
Dil . HCl
× 100
where Met-C-dots refer to the drug-BSC-dot conjugate, amount of metformin stands for the unbound amount of model drug (here Metformin) present in the system.

In vitro drug release study was carried out at 26 °C ± 0.5 °C by Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP) Dissolution Test Apparatus Type 2 (paddle method). The cumulative release was monitored in three conditions; one is in drastic acidic condition (pH 2.1), another one is psychological pH (7.4) and last one is alkaline condition (pH 10.8). In a brief, the 10 mL of the BSC-dots dispersion in water was taken in dialysis bag (MWCO 100–200 Da) and immerged into a 500 mL glass beaker. The system temperature was kept constant around 26 °C ± 0.5 °C. The set up was stirred very mildly at 40 rpm throughout the experiment. At definite intervals, 1.5 mL of the aliquot was taken for measuring UV–visible spectrum at 233 nm. For control experiment, similar kind of experiment was carried out instead of using BSC-dots. In control condition, pure drug solution was taken in dialysis bag and stirred in a similar fashion followed by meaning the release. The cumulative drug release (%R) was calculated as per using the following formula;
% R = [(Total absorbance – Initial absorbance)/Total absorbance] × 100.
Here, the total and initial absorbance signifies the absorbance value at different time intervals and initial time respectively.

The morphology and size of the BSC-dots were studied by TEM and AFM analysis. The TEM image (Fig. 1a) showed that the BSC-dots were spherical and dispersed well with an average size of 3.2 nm. Fig. 1b depicted that these C-dots size spans narrowly from 2.1 nm to 4.2 nm. The AFM micrographs (Fig. 1c) on a silicon substrate showed the spherical morphology of the BSC-dots and the corresponding topographic height profile was shown in Fig. 1d.

The surface composition and structure of the as-prepared BSC-dots were investigated. The FT-IR spectrum of the BSC-dots (Fig. 2a) unveiled distinct absorption bands at 1098 and 1353 cm−1 ascribed to the CB and BO stretching vibrations, respectively [41,42]. The broad peak appeared at around 3586 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra attributed to the stretching vibrations of COH bonds. In addition the peaks occurred at 1705, 1445, and 687 cm−1 because of CO stretching vibration, CH2 bending vibrations, and CS stretching vibrations of the BSC-dots, respectively. Peak at 767 cm−1 assigned to the out of plane bending vibration of benzene which suggests the presence of aromatic rings at the surface of the BSC-dots [23,26,43].

To explore more details about the elemental content and configuration of the B and S doped C-dots the XPS characterization was carried out. The full range XPS (Fig. 2b) study of the BSC-dots clearly revealed four typical peaks at 167.64 eV, 191.81 eV, 284.11 eV, and 531.94 eV which were assigned to the characteristic binding energy signals of S2p, B1s, C1s, and O1s, respectively. The core level peaks of C1s indicated (Fig. 2c) peaks at 283.81 eV, 284.66 eV, and 286.11 eV which were attributed to the CB, CC/CC, CO/CS bonds, respectively [44,45]. The high resolution B1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 190.97 eV (BO), 191.67 eV(B − C bond) which are well in agreement with the of XPS C1s and FT-IR data [46]. The FT-IR and XPS results confirmed that B, and S atoms were successfully doped in BSC-dots and some associated functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl groups) were present on the surface. The 1H NMR spectrum of the BSC-dots was examined in D2O. The NMR spectrum showed signals in different chemical environments. The signals in the range 3–5.2 ppm were attributed to the (Fig.S1) existence of protons attached with hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups at the surface of the as prepared BSC-dots [26]. Signal found in the region 6.5–8 ppm were due to for the aromatic or sp2 protons [47]. Furthermore, the elemental compositions of the BSC-dots were studied from EDX data (Fig. S2). The mass and atomic ratios of carbon (C): boron (B): oxygen (O): sulphur (S) were found to be 66.49: 19.22: 13.96: 0.33 and 67.54: 21.69: 10.65: 0.12, respectively.

To further explore the optical properties of the BSC-dots, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic characterizations were studied. BSC-dots exhibited absorption peak (Fig. 3a) at 225 nm indicating the π to π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbon. In addition to the π to π* transition at 225 nm, the broad absorption peak located at about 281 nm ascribed to the n to π* transition of the CO band [48]. The inset of Fig. 3a displayed the aqueous solution of the BSC-dots under visible light (left) and under UV illumination (right). The C-dots solution was yellow, transparent and clear under visible light and exhibited bright bluish green fluorescence under UV light which is strong enough to be seen with naked eyes. The emission spectrum of the BSC-dots was studied at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths. With increasing excitation wavelengths, red shift in the emission spectra of BSC-dots from 413 to 524 nm was observed (Fig. 3b), accompanied by reduction of the fluorescence intensity. The photoluminescent (PL) spectrum showed a maximum peak located at 470 nm when excited at 380 nm. This observation revealed that the fluorescence of BSC-dots was dependent on the excitation wavelength [49]. This bathochromic shift was generated mainly due to different distributions of emissive trap sites on each C-dots, different size (quantum effect),) radiative recombination of excitons, and free zigzag sites. The PL properties of C-dots always influenced by the size and the presence of functional groups present at the C-dots surface, which effects the excited state energy distribution, the charge separation, and/or the confinement of electrons and holes in the C-dots and their radiative recombination [47,50,51]. Besides their strong downconversion property, the as-prepared BSC-dots revealed excellent upconversion PL. As manifested in Fig. 3c, the BSC-dots were excited by long wavelength in the range 680 to 800 nm and the upconverted emissions were obtained in short wavelength (from 436 to 503 nm). This upconversion property of the BSC-dots was ascribed to the multiphoton active process [52], in which two or more photons absorbed simultaneously leads to anti-Stokes type emission i.e. emission at a shorter wavelength than the excitation [53,54]. A schematic representation of the normal PL and upconverted PL was represented in Fig. 3d. Above result reflected that BSC-dots can be employed as efficient energy-transfer component in biomedical field or photocatalyst design for energy and environmental related applications. The PL spectra of the BSC-dots depicted in Fig. 4a clearly showed that the fluorescence of the BSC-dots was dependent on the concentration. The intensity of the BSC-dots firstly increases with the increasing concentration but at high concentration (2.1 mg/mL) PL intensity decreases. This observation may be due to the increasing interaction among the high amount of polar functional groups assisted to form agglomeration (schematic view in Fig. 4b) at high concentration [47,55]. The fluorescence response of the BSC-dots (Fig. 4c) increased from 3 to 7 as the pH increases, after that the intensity remained almost constant in the range 7–11. Further, increment in the pH resulted in reduction in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence decay profile of the BSC-dots was represented in Fig. 4d which was fitted to a biexponential function using the eq. Y(t) = A 1exp(−t / τ 1) + A 2exp(−t / τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 were the fractional contributions of decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2, respectively. This behavior attributed to the presence of two different emissive sites. The average lifetime of the BSC-dots was calculated to be 6.67 ns which was comparable to studies reported elsewhere [18]. The short lifetime of the BSC-dots unveiled the radiative recombination nature of excitations. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY of the BSC-dots was measured to be 14.64%. The strong luminescence property ascribed to the quantum confinement effect of emissive energy traps to the C-dots surface [56]. The optical property, particle size, and application of C-dots derived from different sources were tabulated in Table 1 . These results tabulated in Table 1 explained that the as-prepared BSC-dots doped with B and S revealed quite comparable and/or better optical properties and applications than the other reported studies. This good performance was mostly reflected to the stable surface functional group at the surface of the C-dots derived from kappa carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid.

Stability of the C-dots is a significant feature to contemplate for several biomedical applications. To examine the photostability of the BSC-dots, the luminescence property of the as-prepared BSC-dots was compared with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) which is commercially used as fluorescence dye. Under continuous UV excitation, decrease in fluorescence intensity of the FITC was observed to 30% of its original value whereas our BSC-dots acted to have a good photostability with stable fluorescence intensity for 60 min (Fig.S3). The stable luminescence property against photobleaching might be caused due to the occurrence of stable functional groups on the surface of the BSC-dots [62]. Furthermore, the BSC-dots unveiled outstanding stability over 60 days without any degradation as shown in Fig. 5a. The fluorescence of the BSC-dots was remained almost same after 2 months as depicted in Fig. 5b. The photostability of the BSC-dots was studied in different physiological conditions such as pH 7.4, 0.15 mM NaCl, 10 mM tris-HCl. The fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots does not affect by these conditions as displayed in Fig. 5c. Moreover, under continuous irradiation of UV light for 180 min the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots scarcely deteriorated (Fig. 5d). The effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots was studied within the temperature range 20 to 50 °C. As depicted in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots was hardly affected by temperature. Ionic strength is one of the key variables which influence chemical reaction. The affect of ionic strength was examined taking NaCl as reference. The NaCl concentration was varied from 0 to 200 mM, and it was noted from Fig. 5f that ionic strength had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots. All these results ensured that BSC-dots endowed excellent photostability which is very essential for application in biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between the C-dots and Glucose
ce:section.ce:para: One interesting facts about boronic acid is that they can reversibly form stable boronate complexes with cis-diols. Therefore, the as prepared BSC-dots functionalized with boronic acid can act as a promissing aspirant for glucose sensing. 2.1 mM glucose was introduced to the BSC-dots and as seen in the Fig. 6a the dispersed C-dots aggregate and form large assembly. Further to examine such assembly was produced by glucose instead of drying on grid, RLS was employed to get idea about the interaction courses. The RLS study (Fig. S4) showed that with the increase of glucose addition the RLS response become stronger. These result certainly validated that the BSC-dots can form assemble in solution with the assistance of glucose. Two pair of cis-diols units present in glucose can covalently bridge with the BSC-dots and produce large assemblies. Generation of C-dots assembly caused quenched (Fig. 6b) fluorescence which is considered as the surface quenching states induced mechanism [63].

The sensing design of glucose-mediated nanoparticle assembly has been demonstrated by two groups of researchers, via boronic acid functionalization of graphene quantum dots [64] and CdTe/ZnTe/ZnS [63] as the signal readout, respectively. Moreover, these two types of functionalized nanoparticles were acquired through conventional two step method, viz. first nanoparticle synthesis and followed by introduction of boronic acid groups on the nanoparticle surface by EDC conjunction method. Whereas, the present study designed fabrication of C-dots as the one-step synthesis-modification and integration approach. These outcomes described that the analyte-induced assembly of boronic acid functionalized nanoparticles is a common behavior irrespective of nanoparticles and functionalization technique. Unlike CdTe/ZnTe/ZnS nanodots [63], the emission of the as prepared BSC-dots did not showed bathochromic shift throughout the course of quenching, which might be due to the photoluminescene of C-dots were less size-dependent [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions effecting for determination of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get the best sensing response, the experimental conditions need to be optimized. At first the effect of pH on the sensing of glucose was investigated. Fig.S5 depicted that at weak acidic environment, the quenching efficiency of glucose was low. The quenching efficiency became obvious as the pH increases after 7 and attained maximum at pH 7.5. On account of the interaction between glucose and boronic acid, basic condition is favorable as the formation of boronate complexes is a release proton reaction [66,67]. Further, the zeta potential of the BSC-dots was augmented (−10.9 to −18.8) as the pH was tuned from 7.0 to 8.0. This result suggested that at high pH condition electrostatic interaction between the BSC-dots increases which hindered the assembly of the BSC-dots. Considering the balance between the above two effects, a weak basic environment (pH 7.5) was chosen for the sensing. Reaction time was another factor also influenced the quenching. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence quenching attained balance at 50 min of reaction for various concentrations of the glucose, which is smaller than the other sensing systems reported elsewhere [68]. Schematic illustration for working principle of BSC-dots binding to glucose molecule was represented in Fig. 6d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of BSC-dots for glucose sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimized conditions, we monitored the changed in emission spectra of the BSC-dots in the presence of different concentration of glucose (Fig. 7a). The introduction of glucose into the BSC-dots sensing system greatly affect the emission of BSC-dots. As the concentration of glucose increases within the micromolar range, the fluorescence intensity of the BSC-dots gradually decreases. The quenching mechanism of the BSC-dots can be better understood by Stern-Volmer analysis. Dynamic quenching is originated from collisional interaction between the quencher and the fluorophore and can be described by Stern-Volmer equation as follows [69].
(2)
F 0
/ F =
τ 0
/ τ = 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
where, F 0 - initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of a quencher and F - fluorescence intensity in the presence of a quencher. τ0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively. k q - the rate constant of quenching (dynamic) and [Q] - quencher concentration. Another type of quenching, namely static quenching arises when a non-fluorescent complex generated between the fluorophore and the quencher. Static quenching the can be described by Stern-Volmer equation as follows.
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where, K SV is the static quenching constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorescent probe for glucose
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the performance of a fluorescent chemosensor, selectivity is an important parameter. We investigated the influence of glucose analogues viz. fructose, lactose, maltose, and sucrose for the quenching of BSC-dots. Glucose and its analogues of 140 μM concentrations were treated with BSC-dots and the variation in fluorescence intensity was recorded under identical conditions. The quenching effect of the four sugars viz. fructose, lactose, maltose, and sucrose were negligible as depicted in Fig. 7c. This result demonstrated that these BSC-dots could effectively detect glucose from other saccharides. Such good selectivity was originated due to the stereostructure properties of glucose. As depicted in Fig. 7d, these substances endowed very slight effects of fluorescent improvement or quenching of the BSC-dot fluorescence. The mixture contained all the substances (except glucose) presented in the Fig. 7d including glutathione, dopamine, lysine, tyrosine, phenylalanine, glutamic acid, serine, aspartic acid, valine, proline, tryptophan, glycine, histidine, methionine, threonine, cysteine, alanine, arginine, and, glutamine. Some of these substances showed fluorescence quenching and the others showed fluorescence enhancement of the BSC-dots (as shown in Fig. 7d). The mixture unveiled the cumulative effect resulting fluorescence quenching. The exclusive response of glucose based on the crosslinking of glucose by the particular reaction of four cis-diols unit with boronic acid groups of the BSC-dots. The absence of additional cis conformational diol unit in four analogues unlike glucose and in these coexisting substances results in, they couldn't cross-link the BSC-dots and lead to the corresponding aggregation induced fluorescent quenching [77,78].

For practical application of glucose detection, a paper stripe based test was designed. Whatmann no.40 filter papers were soaked in a BSC-dots solution for 10 min and then kept for air dried. Under UV irradiation, the paper stripes displayed bluish green emission. These paper stripes further soaked in a solution containing glucose and air dried. The quenching in fluorescence of the paper stripes were observed under UV illumination. The resulted paper stripes were used as the probe for the detection of glucose (Fig. 7e). BSC-dots tethered paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different biological substances were also shown in Fig. 7f. This results clearly illustrated that paper based test stripe can be successfully used for visual detection of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BSC-dots for determination of glucose in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the reliability of the BSC-dots based fluorescent sensor for glucose detection, this nanoprobe was applied to detect glucose in human serum samples. The BSC-dots was added to pretreated serum samples and incubated for 50 min and then fluorescence intensities were measured. As shown in Table S1, the recoveries of glucose were found to be in the range of 97–102.4%. This result illustrated that BSC-dots were feasible for the practical detection of glucose.

The metformin molecule has been loaded onto BSC-dots by means of physical mixing method. Drug loading and release behaviour of metformin was evaluated via UV visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. In Fig. 8a, pristine drug C-dots and drug loaded C-dots have been assessed via UV visible spectra. For pure metformin the absorption peak was depicted at 233 nm. For pure BSC-dots the characteristic peak was at 225 nm. But for drug loaded BSC-dots the characteristic peak of BSC-dots was broadened which might be the merge of metformin and BSC-dots. It could be approve of drug binding over C-dots surface. As metformin has polar amine ends, it has enormous proneness to bind with BSC-dots surface functionalities. The drug binding to BSC-dots was also reinforced by their fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 8b. Metformin bonded BSC-dots sample has characteristic peak at around 465 nm. But after several hours the consecutive fluorescence spectra showed gradual increment in fluorescence intensity of the drug loaded C-dots sample. These data also support the drug binding and release behaviour of the specimen. Initially when drug molecules where attached to the surface of BSC-dots, the surface passivation effect become so stringent the fluorescence behaviour was quietly suppressed. But, with time the drug molecules were released from the BSC-dots surface which imply sustained or timed release of metformin. After 72 h the drug loaded specimen show almost similar pattern of pure BSC-dots fluorescence spectra. From this result the percentage drug loading efficiency also can be evaluated and which was found to be 71.6%.

The timed release plot of drug from the BSC-dots was carried out by UV visible spectra after transforming the absorption value into drug concentration. The effect of pH was also accounted to assess the behaviour of drug release in various pH medium. The cumulative release was plotted against time and shown in Fig. 8. At the physiological pH the drug release was depicted in Fig. 8(d–g). For control experiment Pristine drug solution in dialysis bag was stirred as before. For pristine drug release, the plot shows steep rise having high value of slope which implied fast release of drug molecules. This fast release of drug is terminologically called burst release phenomenon. For control experiment the drug release was around 92%. The cumulative release of drug loaded BSC-dots also support the binding of drug molecules to BSC-dots surface that's why it can be inferred that C-dots could be exploited as Nano Drug Delivery vehicle. Besides, the drug delivery was performed in low and high pH conditions. At low pH environment the surface ligands of C-dots had been protonated and at the same time the amine groups present in metformin was also protonated. The intermolecular hydrogen bonding between surface ligands of BSC-dots and metformin could not be taken place. Similar kind of behaviour was also occurred at alkaline condition but the difference is that in alkaline condition the surface ligands where deprotonated and there would be repulsive force of interaction between deprotonated surface ligands and amine groups' lone pair of electrons in metformin. Another implication of this situation also could be supported the value of zeta potential as calculated previously. The zeta potential value is lying around pH 7 that's why the drug release behaviour was most significant in pH 7.4 that is the physiological pH of human body. The surface chemistry drug loading interaction integration has been graphically illustrated in Fig. 8c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data fittings of drug release to model equations
ce:section.ce:para: The drug release plot with respect to time was taken for data fitting. First 60% data from the release profile had been fitted in Donbrow-Samuelov (1980) zero order kinetics model, Higuchi model (1963) and Korsmeyer-Peppas model (1983). The time to release plot data has been channelized through Leverberg-Marquardt (L-M) algorithm (Origin 8.5® Software). The respective model equations are given below;

Donbrow-Samuelov (1980) zero order kinetics model,
m Dt
=
m De
+
K 0
t

Higuchi model,
m Dt
=
m De
+
K H
t
1 / 2

Korsmeyer-Peppas model,
F D
=
m Dt
m De
=
K KP
t n
where mDt and mDe were the amount of drug released at definite time t and at infinity. Infinity corresponds to the drug release at equilibrium condition. K0, KH, and KKP are the related constants of those respective equations. In Korsmeyer-Peppas model, n signifies the structural and geometrical character of the drug dosage which sometimes called the exponent of diffusion. The value of n is related to the mechanism of diffusion. For Anomalous Fickian diffusion system the diffusion exponent lies in the range of 0.5. This means the release data resembles with the spherical shaped non swellable particles having polydisperse nature. For model parameters the zero order Kinetic model and Higuchi model are not supportive because of their fluctuations. Those data were fitted Peppas model had been taken granted for drug release. In all the fittings the confidence limit where calculated of at least 95%. All the fitted plot has been shown in Fig. 8d–g. The peculiarity for control experiment was due to the burst release phenomenon. Hence, control experiment does not obey any drug release model. The drug release data also has an obvious impact to support the boronic acid surface decorated C-dots as nano-vector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the BSC-dots nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(a) represents to the in vitro cell growth study of human osteosarcoma (MG63) in presence of BSC-dots and in tissue culture plate (TCP). The cell viability was assessed after measuring the cell grown in presence of BSC-dots. The viability is a direct reflection of cell metabolism. The quantitative estimation corroborated the MTT assay of the BSC-dots showed cell count of ~4.97 ± 0.74 × 105 and ~5.04 ± 0.69 × 105 for control and in presence of BSC-dots, respectively on their 5th day of study. The almost identical quantity in cell growth could support non-toxic nature of the prepared BSC-dots. After 5th day of study, the adaptation of perfect cell morphology implied the desirable cell growth and attachment in presence of BSC-dots nanoparticles (Fig. 9b,c).

Hemocompatibility is the extent of rupturing of RBCs (erythrocytes) and burst release of hemoglobin from inside out. As our prepared BSC-dots have been proposed to be used in drug delivery vehicle, the extent of hemoglobin has to be measured. The release of hemoglobin has been quantified after calculating the supernatant UV absorbance values. In PBS medium there were no lysis of RBCs taken place which also has been evidenced in the photograph inset of Fig. 9d. For the BSC-dots containing samples there were no such drastic effect of hemolysis seen as shown in Fig. 9d. But in case of Triton X-100 the RBCs were ruptured and release of hemoglobin cannot be ruled out. As a result, for Triton X-100 the sample was red in colour. The extent of hemolysis was calculated of 3.68% for 700 μg/mL sample which was the highest percentage of hemolysis for our samples. This data is comparable to ISO/TR 7406 which is granted for the standard bio-based specimens. Thus, it can be concluded that the prepared BSC-dots can be utilized as biomaterial due to its negligible toxicity and good biocompatibility.

In summary, phenyl boronic acid and kappa carrageenan were strategically used to fabricate B and S co-doped C-dots with superior photoluminescence properties. The prepared BSC-dots unveiled excellent photostability and tolerance towards different environments. The BSC-dots presented high selectivity and sensitivity for glucose which was successfully applied for blood glucose assay. The present glucose sensing motif was obtained by a single step method without any surface modification. The high sensitivity, low detection limit 1.7 μM, wide linear response (0–210 μM) and cost-effective nature of these BSC-dots based nanoprobe endowed them as an efficient quantitative monitoring of glucose by enriching the path of non-enzymatic glucose sensing. Also, the BSC-dots were used as a vehicle of antidiabetic drug Metformin delivery. The C-dots loaded drug was well demonstrated pH controlled release explaining three different models. The MTT and hemolysis assay revealed good biocompatibility of this nanoprobe. The present work not only offered a new thinking for the fabrication of chemosensor but also well showed the path of blood sugar diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.03.224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global estimates of the prevalence of diabetes for 2010 and 2030

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Bopp, U. Zellweger, D. Faeh, Routine data sources challenge international diabetes Federation extrapolations of national diabetes prevalence in Switzerland, Diabetes Care (2011) DC_110157.
